cards.front: What are the three main array types in C++?
cards.back: std::vector (dynamic array), std::array (fixed-size array), and C-style arrays (fixed-size, legacy).

cards.text: Prefer {{c1::std::vector}} for dynamic arrays and {{c2::std::array}} for fixed-size arrays. Avoid {{c3::C-style arrays}} in modern C++

cards.concept: Key differences between std::array and C-style arrays
cards.explanation:
std::array advantages:
  • No array decay to pointers
  • Has size() member function
  • Supports assignment and comparison
  • Works with at() for bounds checking
  • Better constexpr support
C-style arrays should be avoided.

cards.front: What is the most problematic aspect of C-style arrays?
cards.back: Array decay - C-style arrays decay to pointers when passed to functions, losing size information. This makes them unsafe and hard to use correctly.

cards.text: For strings, prefer {{c1::std::string}} or {{c2::std::string_view}} over C-style strings (char arrays with null terminators)

cards.front: What are the main dangers of C-style strings?
cards.back: Buffer overflows (no bounds checking), must manually manage null terminator, easy to forget size, pointer confusion with == and =, manual memory management.

cards.text: To store references in arrays, use {{c1::std::reference_wrapper}} with the helper functions {{c2::std::ref()}} and {{c3::std::cref()}}

cards.front: What does ptr + n do in pointer arithmetic?
cards.back: Advances the pointer by n elements (n * sizeof(type) bytes), not n bytes. This allows navigating through arrays efficiently.

cards.concept: Using enumerations for type-safe array indexing
cards.explanation:
Pattern for enum-based array access:
  • Define enum with values for each index
  • Add a count/max enumerator at the end
  • Use count to size the array
  • Cast enum values to std::size_t for indexing
This provides type safety and self-documenting code.
examples: enum class Color { red, green, blue, count };

cards.front: How do you create a 2D std::array?
cards.back: Use nested std::array: std::array<std::array<int, cols>, rows>. The outer array represents rows, inner arrays represent columns.

cards.text: To pass std::array efficiently to functions, use {{c1::pass by const reference}}: void func(const std::array<int, 5>& arr)

cards.concept: Modern C++ array best practices summary
cards.explanation:
Key takeaways for arrays in modern C++:
  • Use std::vector when size is runtime-determined or needs to change
  • Use std::array when size is compile-time constant
  • Avoid C-style arrays and C-style strings
  • Use std::string for string manipulation
  • Pass containers by const reference
  • Use at() when you need bounds checking
